cards.q: List the outline of this lecture.
cards.a: Introduction and definitions; epidemiology; aetiology; risk factors and host factors; clinical features; differential diagnosis; investigations; treatment; complications; prognosis; and prevention.

cards.q: What complicates the diagnosis of neonatal sepsis?
cards.a: The frequent presence of non-infectious conditions that resemble sepsis, especially in preterm infants (e.g. RDS, CHD); the absence of optimal or definitive diagnostic tests; and viral and fungal infections in newborns that mimic bacterial infections.

cards.q: What is 'Rule Out Sepsis Syndrome' (ROSS)?
cards.a: Because neonatal sepsis is a high-risk disease, clinicians are compelled to empirically administer antibiotics to infants with risk factors and/or signs of suspected sepsis.

cards.q: Where does neonatal sepsis rank as a cause of neonatal mortality?
cards.a: It is the 3rd leading cause, after asphyxia and LBW/prematurity.

cards.q: What are the top 4 diagnoses in most newborn units?
cards.a: Asphyxia, neonatal sepsis, preterm/LBW, and jaundice.

cards.q: What proportion of neonatal mortality and of first-week deaths is due to neonatal sepsis?
cards.a: 13% of all neonatal mortality, and 42% of deaths in the first week of life.

cards.q: What proportion of neonatal deaths in Nigeria does neonatal sepsis explain?
cards.a: About a quarter (25%).

cards.q: What are the risks of overuse of antibiotics in neonatal sepsis?
cards.a: Increased antibiotic resistance; negative effects of altering the microbiome; an increased rate of serious necrotising enterocolitis; and increased mortality.

cards.q: Define bacteraemia.
cards.a: The presence of viable bacteria in the blood.

cards.q: Define septicaemia.
cards.a: Systemic infection confirmed by the presence of actively multiplying bacteria in the blood.

cards.q: Define neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: A clinical syndrome characterised by systemic signs of infection (the infant is looking ill), accompanied by bacteraemia, in the first 28 days of life.

cards.q: Define suspected sepsis.
cards.a: Regardless of whether there is a clinical symptom or not, the presence of sepsis risk factors in the baby, or findings suggesting sepsis in follow-up.

cards.q: Define clinical sepsis.
cards.a: Clinical and laboratory findings are present, but there is failure to show the causative microorganism.

cards.q: Define proven sepsis.
cards.a: Clinical and laboratory findings are present, with demonstration of the pathogenic microorganism in cultures taken from a sterile field; or a positive blood culture or PCR in the presence of clinical signs and symptoms of infection.

cards.q: What is required to diagnose coagulase-negative sepsis?
cards.a: Two positive blood cultures, or one positive blood culture plus a positive CRP.

cards.q: Define probable sepsis.
cards.a: Presence of signs and symptoms of infection plus at least two abnormal laboratory results, when blood culture is negative.

cards.q: Define possible sepsis.
cards.a: Presence of clinical signs and symptoms of infection plus raised CRP or IL-6/IL-8 level, when blood culture is negative.

cards.q: What is SIRS, and how does it relate to sepsis?
cards.a: SIRS is the physiologic response to inflammation from any source including infection; sepsis = SIRS + focus of infection.

cards.q: Why is SIRS limited in neonates?
cards.a: Because many neonates with sepsis lack a specific focus and signs, especially preterms; and SIRS has not made early diagnosis realizable because of absence of specific symptoms and the suboptimal diagnostic value of laboratory findings.

cards.q: How many SIRS criteria are needed, and what is the extra requirement?
cards.a: At least two of the four criteria, one of which must be abnormal temperature or leukocyte count.

cards.q: State the temperature criterion for SIRS.
cards.a: Core temperature > 38.5 °C or < 36 °C.

cards.q: State the tachycardia and bradycardia criteria for SIRS.
cards.a: Tachycardia — mean heart rate 2 SD above normal for age in the absence of external stimulus. Bradycardia — mean heart rate < 10th centile (provided no external vagal stimulus, beta-blockers or CHD), or unexplained bradycardia of > 30 minutes.

cards.q: State the respiratory and leukocyte criteria for SIRS.
cards.a: Mean respiratory rate 2 SD above normal for age (40–60/min); and leukocyte count elevated or depressed for age, or 10% immature neutrophils.

cards.q: Define severe sepsis.
cards.a: Sepsis plus one of: cardiovascular organ dysfunction; acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS); or ≥ 2 organ dysfunctions (respiratory, renal, neurologic, haematologic or hepatic). Or sepsis plus signs of end organ damage or hypoperfusion.

cards.q: List the signs of end organ damage.
cards.a: Low PaO2/FiO2 (acute lung injury); lack of bowel sounds (ileus); oliguria; increased total bilirubin; and thrombocytopenia.

cards.q: List the signs of hypoperfusion or cardiovascular dysfunction.
cards.a: Increased lactic acid; low blood pressure; decreased capillary refill; and change in mental status.

cards.q: Define septic shock.
cards.a: Severe sepsis plus sepsis-induced hypotension that is refractory to fluid resuscitation.

cards.q: How does the incidence of neonatal sepsis vary?
cards.a: Across regions; across centres within the same region; and from time to time within the same centre. It is commoner in developing than developed nations, and worse in preterms than terms; prevalent organisms and antibiotic sensitivity also vary from time to time.

cards.q: Define early onset neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Symptoms occurring at less than 72 hours — various cut-offs from 48 hours to 7 days exist, but most epidemiological studies use 72 hours.

cards.q: Define late onset and very late onset neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Late onset — cases diagnosed on the 4th to 30th days of life. Very late onset — cases diagnosed in infants hospitalized in the NICU from the first 30 days of life until discharge.

cards.q: Where do early onset and late onset pathogens come from?
cards.a: Early onset pathogens are related to the maternal genital tract, with Group B Streptococcus, and the delivery area; late onset pathogens reflect contamination at a later date and nosocomial infection.

cards.q: Why is the classification different in developing countries?
cards.a: Because similar organisms are usually found before and after 72 hours; most deliveries at home, by TBAs and in maternity centres are infected with community-acquired pathogens, and unclean delivery practices lead to infection with nosocomial organisms early in life. Hence classification into community acquired and hospital acquired.

cards.q: List the causes of early onset sepsis in developed countries.
cards.a: Group B Streptococcus 43–58%, E. coli, other gram-negative bacteria, S. aureus, coagulase-negative Staphylococcus, and L. monocytogenes.

cards.q: List the causes of late onset sepsis in developed countries.
cards.a: Coagulase-negative Staphylococcus, E. coli, Klebsiella sp., S. aureus, Candida albicans, Enterococcus sp., P. aeruginosa, and other Candida sp.

cards.q: List the community-acquired causes of neonatal sepsis in developing countries.
cards.a: Klebsiella sp., S. aureus, E. coli, Group B Streptococcus, S. pneumoniae, and Salmonella sp.

cards.q: List the hospital-acquired causes of neonatal sepsis in developing countries.
cards.a: Klebsiella sp., S. aureus, E. coli, coagulase-negative Staphylococcus, Pseudomonas sp., Enterobacter sp., and Candida sp.

cards.q: List the MAJOR maternal risk factors for neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: ROM > 18 hours or PROM; intrapartum maternal fever > 38.0 ºC; chorioamnionitis; malodorous liquor; and sustained fetal heart rate > 160 beats/min.

cards.q: List the fetal/neonatal risk factors.
cards.a: Congenital malformations and preterm/LBW especially VLBW and ELBW — including ectopia vesicae, neural tube defects, cutis aplasia, exomphalos/omphalocoele/gastroschisis, and obstructive problems of GIT, GUT, CVS etc.

cards.q: List the environmental risk factors.
cards.a: Home, TBA, road/taxi/casualty deliveries; nursery and environmental circumstances and practices (poor soap and water use, poor spacing); overcrowding; and poor, inadequate or infrequent hand washing.

cards.q: List the MINOR risk factors for neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Peri/intrapartum maternal fever > 37.5 ºC but < 38.5 ºC; twin gestation; prematurity < 37 weeks; maternal WBC > 15,000; ROM > 12 hours; tachypnoea; maternal GBS colonization; low APGAR < 5 at 1 minute; mode of delivery; and instrumentation and manipulations.

cards.q: List further minor risk factors relating to family and maternal history.
cards.a: Family history of a sibling with systemic bacterial disease under 3 months of age; urinary tract infection; maternal substance abuse; history of recurrent abortion; and poor maternal nutrition.

cards.q: List the labour characteristics that increase risk.
cards.a: Preterm labour; premature or prolonged time between rupture of membranes and delivery; fetal tachycardia without maternal fever, blood loss, hypotension or tachycardia-inducing medication; foul lochia; and rupture of membranes > 12 hours.

cards.q: List the infant characteristics that increase risk.
cards.a: Apgar < 6 at 5 minutes; meconium-stained amniotic fluid; oxygen requirement; respiratory distress; neutropenia; male sex; congenital anomalies causing breakdown of anatomic barriers to infection; and polymorphonuclear leucocytes with intracellular organisms in gastric aspirate.

cards.q: State the stages of sepsis development (seed-soil principle).
cards.a: Skin/mucosa contamination → invasion of skin/mucosa → multiplication of organisms → demonstration of immunocompetence and antibody responses.

cards.q: List the non-specific mechanisms that are poorly developed in the newborn.
cards.a: Skin and mucosal barriers are weak and fragile; neutrophil and monocyte response (chemotaxis) is slow and inadequate; poor opsonin support; hypocomplementaemia; and intracellular killing of ingested bacteria is weak.

cards.q: Describe the deficient intracellular killing mechanisms in the newborn.
cards.a: Oxygen-dependent mechanisms (OH–, O– formation) are poorly developed; and non-oxygen dependent mechanisms are also poorly developed (lysosomal enzymes).

cards.q: Describe the state of specific immunity in the newborn.
cards.a: Humoral and cellular immunity are weak and ineffective, with relative hypo-immunoglobulinaemia.

cards.q: Which scores and tools are used in the diagnosis of neonatal sepsis?
cards.a: Sepsis score, SOFA score, Töllner sepsis score, the Paediatric Committee (PDCO) of the European Medicines Agency (EMA) score, and sepsis calculators for EOS.

cards.q: Describe the nature of early clinical signs in neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: They are non-specific, non-localizing and subtle; they do not distinguish among organisms and are often not organ specific — e.g. meningitis presenting with respiratory distress or diarrhoea. Signs can be rapidly progressive and easily involve multiple organs or systems.

cards.q: List the GENERAL clinical features of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Fever, temperature instability, poor colour, dusky colour, 'not doing well', poor feeding, sclerema, and oedema.

cards.q: List the GASTROINTESTINAL features of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Poor feeding, poor swallowing/sucking, abdominal distension, vomiting, diarrhoea, water brash/excessive salivation and secretions, feed intolerance, increased feed aspirate, and hepatomegaly.

cards.q: List the RESPIRATORY features of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Respiratory distress; increased nasal secretions; dyspnoea (flaring, chest retractions/recession); tachypnoea; bradypnoea/apnoea; noisy breathing (worsening stridor, grunting, wheezing); cheek puffing; and cyanosis.

cards.q: List the RENAL and CARDIOVASCULAR features of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Renal — oliguria, haematuria, dribbling. Cardiovascular — pallor, dusky colour, mottling, cold clammy skin, tachycardia, bradycardia, cyanosis, hypotension, oedema and dysrhythmias.

cards.q: List the CNS features of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Poor activity, weakness, excessive sleep or poor sleep, apnoea, lethargy, irritability, tremors, seizures, reduced primitive reflexes (poor sucking, swallowing, rooting, abnormal Moro), hyporeflexia, hypotonia, irregular respiration, full fontanelle, and high-pitched cry.

cards.q: List the HAEMATOLOGIC and METABOLIC features of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Haematologic — jaundice, pallor, petechiae, purpura, bleeding, ecchymosis, splenomegaly. Metabolic — unexplained hypoglycaemia, hyperglycaemia, acidosis (respiratory or metabolic), and hyponatraemia.

cards.q: List the seven WHO clinical signs indicating neonatal sepsis for immediate referral.
cards.a: Difficulty feeding; convulsions; movement only when stimulated; respiratory rate > 60 per minute; severe chest indrawing; axillary temperature > 37.5 °C or < 35.5 °C; and cyanosis and grunting.

cards.q: List the suspicious clinical signs of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Petechiae and purpura in the setting of haemodynamic instability; fever; cough; hypoxaemia with leukocytosis and pulmonary infiltrates; and a distended tympanitic abdomen with fever and leukocytosis associated with a perforated bowel.

cards.q: List the clinical variables in the criteria for diagnosis of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Temperature instability; heart rate ≥ 180 or ≤ 100 beats/min; respiratory rate > 40 breaths/min plus grunting or desaturations; lethargy or altered mental status; glucose intolerance (plasma glucose > 10 mmol/L); and feed intolerance.

cards.q: State the haemodynamic variables in neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Blood pressure 2 SD below normal for age; systolic pressure < 50 mmHg in a newborn on day 1; and systolic pressure < 65 mmHg in infants ≤ 1 month.

cards.q: State the tissue perfusion variables in neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Capillary refill > 3 seconds, and plasma lactate > 3 mmol/L.

cards.q: List the NEUROLOGIC differential diagnoses of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy, neonatal seizure, and intracranial haemorrhage.

cards.q: List the RESPIRATORY differential diagnoses of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Respiratory distress syndrome especially in preterms; aspiration pneumonia (amniotic fluid or gastric contents); tracheo-oesophageal fistula; and transient tachypnoea of the newborn.

cards.q: List the CARDIOVASCULAR and GASTROINTESTINAL differential diagnoses.
cards.a: Cardiovascular — congenital hypoplastic left heart syndrome and other structural diseases, persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn, and acquired causes such as myocarditis and hypovolaemic or cardiogenic shock. Gastrointestinal — necrotizing enterocolitis and spontaneous GI perforations.

cards.q: List the METABOLIC differential diagnoses of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Hypoglycaemia; adrenal disorders (adrenal haemorrhage and/or insufficiency, CAH); and inborn errors of metabolism (organic acidurias, lactic acidosis, urea cycle disorder).

cards.q: List the HAEMATOLOGIC differential diagnoses of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Vitamin K clotting factor deficiency; neonatal purpura fulminans; immune mediated thrombocytopenia; immune mediated neutropenia; anaemia from other causes; haemolytic disorders (ABO, Rh isoimmunization, G6PD deficiency); and other hereditary clotting disorders.

cards.q: State the principles of management of neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Review clinical features and have a high index of suspicion, balancing risks against features seen; investigate and take necessary culture samples; commence antibiotic treatment immediately, especially with major risk factors; review treatment with clinical progress and laboratory results; and repeat investigations when necessary, since sudden deterioration may occur.

cards.q: What does the sepsis screen consist of?
cards.a: Full blood count, blood cultures, CSF examination, urine cultures, culture of other relevant body fluids or effluents, and chest radiograph if indicated.

cards.q: State the complete blood count findings suggestive of sepsis.
cards.a: White blood cell count decreased < 5,000/mm³ or increased > 34,000/mm³; absolute neutrophil < 1,000/mm³; bands to total neutrophil count ratio > 0.2; and immature to mature neutrophil count ratio > 0.2.

cards.q: When is urinalysis and urine culture indicated?
cards.a: Indicated for late onset sepsis; not useful in early onset sepsis.

cards.q: State the Töllner score interpretation.
cards.a: Less than 5 — normal; 5–10 — suspected; more than 10 points — definite sepsis. It combines clinical and laboratory findings.

cards.q: State the normal neutrophil band to total neutrophil ratio.
cards.a: Less than 0.16 at 12 hours of life, and less than 0.12 after 60 hours.

cards.q: List the acute phase reactants and cut-offs used in neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Elevated micro erythrocyte sedimentation rate; elevated C-reactive protein; elevated high sensitivity CRP > 1 mg/L; and elevated procalcitonin > 2–2.5 ng/mL after postnatal 72 hours.

cards.q: List the cytokines, chemokines and other ancillary markers used.
cards.a: CD11b, CD64, IL-6 and IL-8 as markers of inflammation; also monokines, RANTES, MCP-1 and IP-10; plus haptoglobulin, buffy coat smear, Serum Amyloid A (SAA), and Lipopolysaccharide Binding Protein (LBP).

cards.q: State the other laboratory findings supporting neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Immature neutrophils > 10%; immature:total neutrophil ratio > 0.2; thrombocytopenia < 100,000 × 10⁹/L; CRP > 10 mg/L or 2 SD above normal; procalcitonin > 8.1 mg/dL or 2 SD above normal; IL-6 or IL-8 > 70 pg/mL; and 16S PCR positive.

cards.q: List other investigations used in neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Polymerase chain reaction techniques to recover bacteria; latex agglutination tests; clotting profile; liver function test to rule out bacterial hepatitis; blood glucose; electrolytes; and renal function because of aminoglycosides.

cards.q: In what percentage of neonatal sepsis is blood culture positive?
cards.a: Only 5–10%.

cards.q: When is arterial blood gas indicated?
cards.a: For signs or symptoms of hypoxia.

cards.q: List the indications for lumbar puncture in neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Primary diagnosis of sepsis; positive blood culture; and neurological signs or symptoms.

cards.q: Why are superficial swab cultures not recommended?
cards.a: Cultures from superficial regions such as the axilla, umbilical cord, outer ear canal, nasopharynx and orogastric tubes show poor correlation; routine collection is therefore not recommended.

cards.q: Why are tracheal aspirate cultures not recommended in prolonged intubation?
cards.a: Because of rapid colonization following intubation.

cards.q: Define rational use of antibiotics.
cards.a: The correct use of quality antibiotics, in the right dosage, with wide microbial coverage, for the right duration, via the right mode of delivery, for the right patient.

cards.q: How is nosocomial neonatal sepsis often treated?
cards.a: With vancomycin or fluoroquinolones plus amikacin.

cards.q: State the resistance rates of Klebsiella species given in the lecture.
cards.a: Resistance to gentamicin is about 60–72%, and to 3rd generation cephalosporins 57–66%.

cards.q: How is response to antibiotic treatment monitored?
cards.a: With clinical state, CRP, procalcitonin, and immature/total neutrophil levels.

cards.q: On what is the choice of initial antibiotics based?
cards.a: The sensitivity pattern in the local setting, and review of the response of the patient, especially where laboratory evaluation is poor.

cards.q: State the WHO first line antibiotic recommendation.
cards.a: Penicillin (benzylpenicillin, ampicillin or amoxicillin) plus an aminoglycoside (gentamicin); and then vancomycin or cloxacillin plus an aminoglycoside (amikacin).

cards.q: State the WHO second line antibiotic recommendation.
cards.a: Penicillin or cephalosporin (cefotaxime or ceftriaxone) plus an aminoglycoside (amikacin).

cards.q: List the supportive therapy in neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Correction of anaemia, electrolyte and acid/base imbalances; prevention of hypoxia and hypoglycaemia/hyperglycaemia; maintaining a thermoneutral environment; exchange blood transfusion and buffy coat transfusion; treatment with recombinant cytokines; and management of hypotension and shock.

cards.q: What are the indications for exchange blood transfusion in neonatal sepsis?
cards.a: Sclerema, bacterial hepatitis with high conjugated bilirubin, and leucopenia.

cards.q: Which ancillary treatments are of doubtful use in neonatal sepsis?
cards.a: Treatments that increase the number or function of neutrophils — granulocyte transfusions, granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF), and intravenous immune globulin (IVIG).

cards.q: How does pentoxifylline work, and what was its effect?
cards.a: It reduces TNF-α concentrations associated with sepsis and improves microcirculation; it showed better survival.

cards.q: What intrapartum prophylaxis reduces early onset sepsis, and when is it given?
cards.a: Intrapartum penicillin prophylaxis 2–4 hours before spontaneous vaginal delivery, especially in mothers culture-positive for GBS — this significantly reduces EOS.

cards.q: What eye care and cord care are recommended for prevention?
cards.a: Eye care with 0.5% erythromycin eye ointment stat; and cord care with 4% chlorhexidine gel once daily for 5–7 days.

cards.q: What immediate post-delivery measure is recommended, and for how long?
cards.a: Deliver the baby on the mother's abdomen for skin-to-skin contact for the critical 1 hour.

cards.q: List the remaining preventive measures for neonatal sepsis.
cards.a: Minimise or reduce risk factors; good antenatal care to avoid preterm/LBW and asphyxiated deliveries; regular hand washing to reduce skin/mucosa contamination; early and exclusive breastfeeding; reduce traffic flow in the newborn unit; personalize facilities among newborns on admission; sepsis control in the newborn unit; and rational use of antibiotics.
